name: Gold Foil Printing
shortDescription: Metallic, raised gold/silver finish using heat-stamped foil.
howItWorks: A heated die press transfers a thin layer of metallic or pigmented foil onto paper or card using heat and pressure — the foil only sticks where the heated die actually touches the surface.
whyUsed: Creates a raised, reflective metallic finish that ordinary ink can't achieve — ideal for premium wedding card text, borders, and logos that need to catch the light.

name: UV Printing
shortDescription: Instantly-cured glossy ink with raised, scratch-resistant texture.
howItWorks: UV-curable ink is printed onto the surface and instantly hardened using ultraviolet light instead of air-drying.
whyUsed: Gives vivid, glossy, scratch-resistant spot coatings and prints cleanly on non-porous surfaces like acrylic and coated board — great for raised-texture highlights.

name: Screen Printing
shortDescription: Thick, opaque, durable ink layer pushed through a mesh stencil.
howItWorks: Ink is pushed through a fine mesh stencil with a squeegee; ink only passes through the open areas of the stencil onto the material below.
whyUsed: Lays down a thicker, more opaque, longer-lasting ink layer than digital printing — ideal for packaging boxes, tags, and solid colour blocks that need to survive handling.

name: Die-Cut Works
shortDescription: Precision custom shapes cut using a steel die press.
howItWorks: A custom-shaped steel die is pressed through stacked sheets to cut precise, repeatable shapes — windows, pockets, rounded corners, intricate cut-outs.
whyUsed: Allows complex, brand-specific shapes (card pockets, packaging fold lines, label outlines) that a straight blade can't replicate accurately at volume.

name: Digital Colour Printing
shortDescription: Direct-from-file colour printing — no plates needed.
howItWorks: A digital press prints directly from a digital file with no printing plates, building the image dot-by-dot in CMYK plus extra colours.
whyUsed: No plate setup cost or time — the most efficient choice for short runs, variable data, proofs, brochures, and calendars where speed and flexibility matter.

name: Cold Lamination (Matt | Gloss)
shortDescription: Room-temperature plastic film bonding for protection and finish.
howItWorks: A thin plastic film is bonded onto the printed sheet using pressure and adhesive at room temperature — no heat involved.
whyUsed: Protects ink from scuffing and moisture and adds a matt or gloss finish without heat that could warp delicate or foiled/UV-printed stock.

name: Thermal Lamination (Gloss | Matt | Soft-Touch | Anti-Scratch)
shortDescription: Heat-fused film for a strong, durable, premium-feel finish.
howItWorks: A plastic film is fused onto the sheet using heated rollers, melting the adhesive layer for a permanent, edge-to-edge bond.
whyUsed: Gives a stronger, longer-lasting seal than cold lamination — soft-touch film adds a velvety feel for premium covers, anti-scratch suits frequently-handled items like menus and diary covers.

name: Perfect Binding
shortDescription: Flat-spine binding for brochures and catalogues.
howItWorks: Gathered pages are glued along one edge into a flat-spine cover, the standard paperback-style construction.
whyUsed: Gives brochures and catalogues a clean, professional flat spine that can carry printed text, at a lower cost than hardcover binding.

name: Spiral Binding
shortDescription: Coiled wire/plastic spine through punched holes.
howItWorks: Pages are punched and a continuous coil is wound through the holes, securing all the pages along one edge.
whyUsed: Lets pages lie completely flat and rotate 360° — ideal for calendars, manuals, and notebooks that need to stay open while in use.

name: Hardcover Binding
shortDescription: Rigid board cover wrapped in cloth or printed paper.
howItWorks: Pages are sewn or glued into sections, then cased into a rigid board cover wrapped in cloth, leather-look, or printed paper.
whyUsed: Built to last years of daily handling — the standard choice for diaries and premium presentation books.
